Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Mar-15-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF BRAD BISHOP's Motion For Leave To File Amended Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to file the first amended complaint is granted. The court has a liberal policy in favor of amendment. "The policy favoring amendment is so strong that it is a rare case in which denial of leave to amend can be justified." (Howard v. County of San Diego (2010) 184 Cal.App.4th 1422, 1428.) Here, plaintiff's amendments do not change the tenor of the case, but merely plead additional instances of the same alleged wrongdoing and related causes of action. The Doe defendant that plaintiff seeks to add presumably has been aware of the allegations since the complaint was served. Defendants are free to seek a trial continuance in department 206 before the Presiding Judge if they believe additional discovery is necessary.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)

Motion To Compel Production Of Documents Demand For Jury Trial
Off Calendar Nov-17-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: PLAINTIFF BRAD BISHOP's Motion To Compel Production Of Documents Demand For Jury Trial.

Appearances (via Zoom):

Sangeetha Rao, Esq., Law Office of, (415) 944-7260, for Plaintiff.

William A. Munoz, of FREEMAN MATHIS & GARY, LLP, (916) 272-1423, for Defendants.

Ruling is argued. At conclusion of argument, and having reviewed the pleadings, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: The court has reviewed the papers in support of and in opposition to the motion, as well as the report and recommendation of the judge pro tem following the hearing of October 27, 2023, and adopts it as follows: The motion is denied and defendant's motion for sanctions is granted. While the motion is styled as a motion to compel compliance under CCP Sec. 2031.320, a review of the dispute shows it is not. It is a motion to compel further responses, which carries a 45-day deadline. CCP Sec. 2031.310(c). Rather than claiming that defendant has not produced the documents it promised to produce, plaintiff takes issue with the responses themselves, including defendant's objections and refusals to produce certain documents. There is no dispute that the Motion was made long after the 45-day deadline had passed. The court notes the Decl. of Liam Gaarder-Feingold dated Oct. 16, 2023 at 25, pages 5-6, and notes that one may not re-propound the same discovery in an effort to re-set the 45 day clock. Weil & Brown, et al., CALIFORNIA PRACTICE GUIDE: CIVIL PROCEDURE BEFORE TRIAL 1150.5 (Rutter: 2023). On the merits, as defendant points out, even if the court were to consider this motion one to compel compliance, defendant has produced everything it said it would produce. Defendant's request for sanctions in the amount of $3,022.50 is reasonable and well supported by the Declaration of Mr. Gaarder-Feingold, counsel for defendant. That sum is awarded in favor defendants and against counsel for plaintiff, Sangeetha Rao.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/CEK)
